title: Cat Clear Watery Eye Discharge: Causes and Triage Guide
meta_desc: Learn about clear watery eye discharge in cats, when it is an emergency, and why sharing a photo with your vet can help with a quick diagnosis.
content_html:
TL;DR: Clear, watery eye discharge in cats is typically a low-urgency response to irritants or mild viruses, but you should see a vet if your cat begins squinting or the discharge changes color.

What causes clear, watery eye discharge in my cat?

Clear, watery discharge from a cat's eye, medically known as epiphora, is a common occurrence. It typically indicates that the eye is reacting to a mild irritant, an environmental allergen like dust or pollen, or a viral infection such as Feline Herpesvirus. Unlike thick yellow or green discharge, which suggests a bacterial infection, clear fluid is often the body's way of flushing out the ocular surface.

Is clear watery eye discharge in my cat an emergency?

  • The urgency level for clear, watery discharge is generally Low if your cat is eating, drinking, and behaving normally.
  • Monitor your cat closely for any changes in the discharge, such as it becoming cloudy, yellow, or green.
  • Schedule a veterinary appointment within 24 to 48 hours if your cat begins squinting, pawing at their face, or showing signs of pain.
  • Early intervention is key to preventing further complications like corneal ulcers.

How does providing a photo of my cat's eye help with triage?

  • A clear, well-lit photo allows a professional to look for subtle signs of inflammation.
  • It helps the veterinary team check for foreign objects lodged on the ocular surface.
  • Clinicians can assess the symmetry of the pupils to rule out more serious issues.
  • Visual evidence helps the triage team determine if the issue can be managed at home or if an in-person exam is required immediately.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, clear, watery ocular discharge may be associated with conjunctivitis caused by infectious agents such as feline herpesvirus-1 (FHV-1), Mycoplasma, or Chlamydia psittaci, often starting unilaterally and progressing bilaterally within a week. Diagnosis relies on history, physical examination, conjunctival scrapings and culture, Schirmer tear test, and occasionally biopsy. Cytologic examination of Giemsa-stained conjunctival scrapings is valuable for identifying chlamydiae and mycoplasmas, while PCR of ocular, nasal, or caudal pharyngeal secretions can help identify the causative agent. Treatment is largely symptomatic and supportive, including frequent removal of nasal and ocular discharges. Broad-spectrum antibiotics are indicated to combat secondary bacterial infections, with tetracyclines and fluoroquinolones being particularly effective against C felis and M felis.

Chapter: Infectious Disease, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1482)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No, you should never use human eye drops or medications on a cat without veterinary approval, as some ingredients can be toxic or worsen the condition.

faqs.question: When should I be worried about my cat's watery eye?
faqs.answer: You should seek veterinary care if the eye becomes red, if the cat is squinting constantly, or if the discharge changes color from clear to yellow or green.

faqs.question: How can I clean my cat's watery eye at home?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water, being careful not to touch the eyeball itself.
